fields.description:
## Context

DSO plugin users across NextJS, Flask, and Ruby projects rely on LLM sub-agents for all code changes. Many tasks are mechanical and deterministic (add parameter + update callers, scaffold routes, normalize imports) but handled non-deterministically by LLMs — introducing variance, wasted tokens, and reviewable defects. This epic delivers standalone recipe infrastructure: a registry, execution engine, and initial catalog of 3 recipes that any project can invoke via CLI for deterministic transforms.

The three initial recipe types (add-parameter, scaffold-route, normalize-imports) are the most frequently recurring mechanical tasks across the target stacks. A separate Epic B wires this library into /dso:implementation-plan and /dso:sprint.

## Success Criteria

1. recipe-registry.yaml maps recipe names to language, engine (ts-morph for TypeScript, Rope for Python, Synvert for Ruby), CLI command template, capability description with explicit scope (single-file/multi-file/cross-package), minimum engine version, and installation instructions — verified by JSON schema validation at load time
2. A recipe-executor.sh script accepts a recipe name + parameters, resolves the engine from the registry, validates engine presence + version, executes the CLI command with timeout and git stash rollback on failure, and returns structured JSON result (files_changed, transforms_applied, errors, exit_code) — verified by executing a recipe and parsing the JSON output
3. Recipe parameters are passed via environment variables or argument arrays, never via shell string interpolation — verified by security test with metacharacters in parameters
4. When an engine is not installed, the executor returns a structured degraded status with the missing engine name — verified by test removing engine binary
5. Initial catalog includes add-parameter (cross-file signature + caller update), scaffold-route (framework-specific boilerplate), and normalize-imports (sort + deduplicate) — each with implementations for at least 2 of 3 target languages
6. Each recipe is idempotent — running it twice on the same input produces identical output — verified by double-execution test
7. Determinism: 3 consecutive runs on the same input produce zero-variance output — verified by hash comparison of output files
8. Each recipe is tested against a real open-source codebase fixture (cloned from a public repo representative of the target stack) and asserts semantic correctness — e.g., add-parameter on a codebase with 15+ callers across 8 files correctly updates every caller, verified by the codebase's own test suite passing after the transform. These are external integration tests.

## Approach

A recipes/ directory with recipe-registry.yaml manifest. A recipe-executor.sh script resolves the engine from the registry, validates engine presence + version via pre-flight check, executes with timeout:600000, captures structured JSON output, and rolls back on failure via git stash pop. Each recipe wraps a language-specific engine: ts-morph for TypeScript (cross-file via TS compiler reference graph), Rope for Python (native ChangeSignature with cross-file caller update), Synvert for Ruby (AST pattern matching with 860+ existing recipes). Recipes are testable standalone — no planning or sprint integration in this epic.

## Scenario Analysis (14 surviving)

- Shell timeout (critical): timeout:600000 + git stash rollback on partial transform
- Partial transform on crash (critical): git stash snapshot before execution; rollback on non-zero exit
- Command injection (critical): env var parameterization; no shell string interpolation of recipe parameters
- Post-implementation ordering (high): cleanup before review — enforced in Epic B, not this epic
- Nesting violation on fallback (high): degraded status return, not Task dispatch — no nesting risk
- Engine version skew (high): minimum version in registry; pre-flight version check
- Recipe idempotency (high): registry contract; double-execution test per recipe
- Monorepo resolution (high): scope field in registry (single-file/multi-file/cross-package)
- Capability description mismatch (high): structured scope in capability descriptions
- Engine not on PATH (medium): pre-flight uses exact invocation string from registry
- Schema validation (medium): JSON schema at registry load time
- Output misidentification (medium): per-engine exit code adapter in executor
- Fallback not surfaced (medium): structured degraded status with engine name
- Stale install instructions (low): min version + CI smoke test

## Dependencies

- No hard dependencies (standalone infrastructure)
- Epic B (separate ticket): Wire recipe library into implementation planning and sprint
- Security remediation epic (separate ticket): Security-focused recipe catalog

## Research Findings

### Codemod Engine Selection
- Trigger: Unfamiliar dependency (3 engines)
- Query: Best deterministic codemod engine per language for cross-file transforms
- Sources: github.com/dsherret/ts-morph, github.com/python-rope/rope, github.com/synvert-hq/synvert-ruby
- Key insight: ts-morph and Rope have true cross-file caller resolution via type system / project index. Synvert uses AST pattern matching (not type-resolved) but works well for Rails conventions. OpenRewrite is Java-focused with nascent Python support — not suitable for our stack.

### Planning Intelligence Log

- Web research (Step 2.6): triggered
  - Bright-line conditions: unfamiliar dependency (ts-morph, Rope, Synvert), novel architectural pattern (recipe-driven implementation planning)
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): triggered
  - Scenarios surviving blue team filter: 14
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: none
